---
title: "For Participants"
subtitle: "Everything you need to know before and during the session"
format:
  html: default
  pdf:
    geometry: margin=2cm
    fontsize: 11pt
---

## What to bring

A real piece of code you're working on. It doesn't need to be:

- Finished
- Working correctly
- Well-organised
- In a language your partner knows

If you're not sure what to bring, pick a script you run regularly. There's almost always something to improve, explain, or make clearer.

## The two roles

**Driver** — controls the keyboard and types. Focuses on the immediate task: writing, reading, and editing code.

**Navigator** — watches, thinks ahead, and asks questions. Focuses on the bigger picture: does this approach make sense, are there edge cases, what should happen next?

Switch roles regularly — every 10–15 minutes, or when you reach a natural pause.

## Before you start each round

- Give your partner a 2-minute tour of your code: what does it do, what are you working on, where are you stuck?
- Agree who drives first

## While you're pairing

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="50%"}
**If you're the driver**

- Talk through what you're doing as you type
- Ask the navigator before making big changes
- Don't feel you have to type quickly — thinking out loud matters more
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
**If you're the navigator**

- Ask questions freely — "what does this variable hold?"
- Don't grab the keyboard; say what you'd try and let the driver type it
- Watch for typos and logic errors the driver might miss
:::

::::

**Both of you**

- It's fine not to solve the problem — the conversation is the point
- Messy, unfinished code is normal and expected; no judgement
- If you're stuck, say so — "I have no idea what this does" is a useful thing to say

## Things that often come up in sessions

- A tool or library your partner uses that you haven't seen before
- A different way of approaching a problem you've been stuck on
- Something you've been doing for years that turns out to have a neater solution
- A bug that you spotted the moment you had to explain the code out loud

## Making it a regular practice

A one-off session is a good introduction — but pairing works best as a habit, not an event.

A few things worth trying after today:

- **Find a buddy for your next project**, not just your current one. The best time to pair is at the very start, when you're making structural decisions that are hard to change later.
- **Pair with someone different each time.** A collaborator in a different lab, someone who uses a different language or tool, someone at a very different experience level — each pairing teaches you something the last one didn't.
- **Even occasional pairing helps.** You don't need to pair on every line of code. A 30-minute session at the start of a new analysis, or when you're stuck on something, is enough to get real value.

If you'd like to be matched with a new pairing partner outside of organised events, [get in touch](mailto:gabriel.bernardoharrington@ukdri.ac.uk).

## Further reading and watching

- [**On Pair Programming**](https://martinfowler.com/articles/on-pair-programming.html) — the most thorough practical guide available. Worth reading if you want to go deeper on techniques and common objections.
- [**Scientific research into pair programming**](https://tuple.app/pair-programming-guide/scientific-research-into-pair-programming) — a readable summary of the empirical evidence, if you want to convince a sceptical colleague.
- [**All About Pair Programming (AMA)**](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=R04qyUVo2kE) — Ben Orenstein answers common questions in a live Q&A. Good background watch.
